network.overall_strategy: This network targets threonine through a literature-grounded, cross-environment prebiotic network centered on shared carbon-nitrogen hubs: HCN, acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, and the threonine aminonitrile. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced C1-C3 feedstocks such as formate, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, and concentrating mineral interfaces; surface cyanosulfidic and photochemical chemistry converts HCN into aldehydes and aminonitriles under UV irradiation; biochemical/prebiotic stages complete hydrolysis, reductive amination, and rapid utilization before threonine decomposes. Ten distinct pathways are represented as combinations of experimentally supported routes: cyanosulfidic Strecker entry via lactaldehyde, direct glyceraldehyde Strecker entry, spark-discharge generation of Strecker precursor pools, Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin chemistry, HCN polymer hydrolysate production, speculative-but-literature-anchored aldol/reductive-amination connections, and downstream peptide incorporation. The network explicitly reflects the major literature constraint that threonine is relatively easy to form but thermally fragile, favoring lower-temperature vent margins, UV-exposed pools, and rapid transfer into downstream chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to pyruvate-like keto acid pool

conditions.other: alkaline vent to vent-margin setting; anoxic; H2-rich; low-temperature chimney precipitates near 40-80C favored for product persistence

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed CO2 hydrogenation and carbon-fixation-like iron-sulfur chemistry generates reduced carboxylates including pyruvate; extension to 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate is a connected but less directly demonstrated homologation step.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation on FeS systems under hydrothermal conditions is supported by Cody et al. (2000), while vent mineralogy and low-temperature alkaline chimney relevance are supported by White et al. (2015) and Lane & Martin (2012). Extension to threonine's keto precursor is included as a necessary but partially speculative homologation consistent with Huber & Wachtershauser (2006), Muchowska et al. (2019), and Kaur et al. (2024).

reactions.name: Surface cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of simple aldehydes from HCN

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system with mineral photocatalytic surfaces
conditions.other: UV-irradiated surface pool; phosphate-buffer-compatible cyanosulfidic regime; H2S as reductant

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven reductive homologation of HCN through cyanide photoredox cycling yields formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This directly follows the cyanosulfidic aldehyde-generating chemistry of Ritson & Sutherland (2013) and Patel et al. (2015). TiO2 is included as a plausible co-occurring photocatalytic mineral in UV-exposed pools per Dunn et al. (1981), without replacing the Cu-cyanide photoredox core.

reactions.name: Aldol homologation from glycolaldehyde and formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic or hot-spring pool; borate stabilization of sugars

reactions.mechanism: Aldol/formose-type carbon-carbon bond formation generates glyceraldehyde from lower aldehydes, with borate suppressing uncontrolled rearrangement and stabilizing sugar products.
reactions.reasoning: Glyceraldehyde is a key branch point in Sutherland-type chemistry and aldol/formose chemistry with borate stabilization is consistent with the literature summary and established sugar chemistry; its central role connecting nucleotide and amino-acid precursors is emphasized by Patel et al. (2015) and the branch-point discussion in the research summary. This step is a necessary network connector and moderately inferential rather than directly quantified for the exact substrate pair.

reactions.name: Photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: 2 h UV irradiation; H2S reductant; cyanosulfidic surface pool

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde first forms its cyanohydrin with HCN, then undergoes photoredox reduction under UV/H2S/Cu-cyanide conditions to yield lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This is the experimentally demonstrated key step reported by Ritson & Sutherland (2013), who obtained lactaldehyde from acetaldehyde cyanohydrin in 19% yield after irradiation. Patel et al. (2015) embeds this chemistry in broader cyanosulfidic protometabolism.

reactions.name: Direct Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions in anoxic pool

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine/iminium, followed by cyanide addition to form 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The lactaldehyde-to-threonine-aminonitrile Strecker sequence is the central validated route described by Ritson & Sutherland (2013) and Patel et al. (2015). Mineral surfaces are included for concentration and compatibility with the required surface-agent specification, consistent with clay-mediated concentration in prebiotic pools.

reactions.name: Direct Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes Strecker addition with ammonia and cyanide to produce the same threonine aminonitrile branch point.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) specifically identifies a parallel route in which glyceraldehyde plus NH3 and HCN gives the threonine aminonitrile, directly linking RNA-precursor chemistry to amino acid precursor synthesis.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN/polymeric nitrile reservoir

conditions.catalyst: mineral surfaces under UV and drying-concentration cycles
conditions.other: HCN-rich pool; oligomerization reservoir for later hydrolysis products

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes to diaminomaleonitrile and related polymers that act as a broad precursor reservoir for amino acids upon subsequent processing.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization is a classic prebiotic process and Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) reviewed hydrolysates of HCN polymers yielding 12 proteinogenic amino acids including threonine. DAMN is included as a representative oligomer node.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomer reservoir to amino-acid mixture including threonine and glycine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis of preformed HCN polymers or residues

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic breakdown of nitrile-rich HCN oligomers releases amino acids as part of a complex product spectrum.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of HCN polymers producing threonine is supported by the review of Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013). Product complexity and low selectivity are explicitly acknowledged in that literature.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of lactaldehyde to hydantoin intermediate

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration surfaces with ammonium carbonate-compatible medium
conditions.other: ammonium carbonate / dissolved CO2 conditions; anoxic aqueous pool

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide and ammonia/carbonate equivalents to form a hydantoin thermodynamic sink.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. (2022) experimentally established Bucherer-Bergs-like prebiotic amino acid access through hydantoins for analogous systems, and the deep research report specifically identifies 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin as the expected threonine intermediate from lactaldehyde. This threonine-specific step is literature-anchored but not directly demonstrated, so it remains a necessary extrapolation.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin to threonine
reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin yields threonine/allothreonine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to amino acids is the core outcome of Bucherer-Bergs chemistry as demonstrated by Pulletikurti et al. (2022). The threonine-specific hydrolysis is the expected homologous analogue identified in the research summary and is included as a justified extension.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.other: pH ~8 for Ni/H2 route; alternatively Fe2+ at 70C

reactions.mechanism: The alpha-keto acid undergoes imine formation with ammonia followed by reduction to the alpha-amino acid threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids is directly demonstrated by Muchowska et al. (2019) using Fe2+ at 70C and by Kaur et al. (2024) using H2/Ni at 22C and pH 8. Threonine's exact precursor 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate was not experimentally tested in those studies, so this reaction is explicitly a literature-supported extrapolation.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycine with acetaldehyde to threonine/allothreonine

conditions.other: requires high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance; thermodynamically unfavorable

reactions.mechanism: Reverse threonine-aldol chemistry forms a C-C bond between glycine-derived and acetaldehyde-derived fragments to give threonine and allothreonine.
reactions.reasoning: Fesko (2016) discusses the reversibility of threonine aldolase chemistry and the unfavorable but conceptually simple condensation of glycine with acetaldehyde to give threonine/allothreonine. No purely prebiotic demonstration of this exact reaction has been reported, so this step is explicitly speculative but included because the literature identifies it as a key structural relationship.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde-Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry generates acetaldehyde, which is converted through lactaldehyde to the threonine aminonitrile and then threonine.

pathways.name: Glyceraldehyde direct Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN-derived aldehydes feed sugar homologation to glyceraldehyde, which directly furnishes the threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Lactaldehyde Bucherer-Bergs route
pathways.description: Lactaldehyde enters hydantoin chemistry before hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysate route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes and on hydrolysis releases threonine among a broader amino acid mixture.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal keto-acid reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal mineral chemistry supplies the keto-acid precursor pool, which is reductively aminated under mild prebiotic conditions.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface lactaldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation supplies reduced carbon pools that can feed surface HCN chemistry and funnel into lactaldehyde-mediated Strecker synthesis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface glyceraldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal feedstocks and surface aldehyde chemistry converge on glyceraldehyde, then on the shared threonine aminonitrile hub.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge analogue through aldehyde pool to lactaldehyde branch
pathways.description: The spark-discharge literature indicates in situ production of HCN, NH3, and aldehydes including lactaldehyde precursors; in network form this maps onto the aldehyde pool and lactaldehyde Strecker branch.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge analogue through glyceraldehyde branch
pathways.description: A second discharge-compatible route uses the same HCN/ammonia/aldehyde precursor ensemble but channels through glyceraldehyde to the aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Glycine-acetaldehyde aldol route
pathways.description: A conceptually simple but unfavorable route condenses glycine with acetaldehyde to produce threonine/allothreonine directly.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strongest literature-aligned route. The cyanosulfidic generation of aldehydes from HCN/H2S under UV and conversion of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde closely match Ritson & Sutherland and Patel et al. The weak point is that the pathway stops at threonine aminonitrile rather than explicitly including hydrolysis to threonine within the pathway definition, though the network contains downstream hydrolysis chemistry indirectly.
suggestions: Add an explicit aminonitrile hydrolysis step from threonine aminonitrile to threonine/allothreonine under mild aqueous conditions
Specify Cu(I) cyanide photoredox chemistry or equivalent mineral support in the reaction metadata to better match literature

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Reasonably plausible and supported by the literature statement that glyceraldehyde can provide a direct Strecker entry to threonine aminonitrile. The main concern is the simplified aldol homologation chemistry to glyceraldehyde, which is plausible in a prebiotic sugar network but underspecified and less selective than presented.
suggestions: Clarify whether borate or other sugar-stabilizing minerals are involved in glyceraldehyde generation
Include the hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine as an explicit target-forming step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: A good alternative route grounded in the Bucherer-Bergs logic highlighted in the literature. Formation of a hydantoin from lactaldehyde is chemically sensible, and hydrolysis to threonine is reasonable. However, the network description uses CO2 rather than explicitly ammonium carbonate/bicarbonate conditions, so the Bucherer-Bergs setup is somewhat compressed.
suggestions: Specify ammonium carbonate or bicarbonate-rich conditions rather than free CO2 alone
State that hydantoin hydrolysis likely requires prolonged aqueous heating and may yield diastereomeric mixtures

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is literature-supported at the broad level because HCN polymer hydrolysates can yield amino acid mixtures including threonine. However, it is low-selectivity, poorly controlled, and typically produces complex mixtures with threonine at low abundance. It is acceptable as a background source pathway but weak as a targeted synthesis route.
suggestions: Treat this as a reservoir or side-production pathway rather than a principal threonine route
Add discussion of low selectivity and low yield relative to glycine and aspartate

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest major pathway. Reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine is conceptually justified by analogy to known keto-acid aminations, but the specific threonine precursor has not been directly demonstrated in the cited literature. More problematic is the hydrothermal generation of both pyruvate and 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate in a single step from CO2 and H2, which is speculative and insufficiently justified.
suggestions: Downgrade this route to speculative status explicitly
Add intermediate chain-elongation steps from pyruvate or acetyl-like precursors to 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate
Align reductive amination conditions with Fe2+ at moderate temperatures or Ni/H2 at room temperature and pH 8 as in the literature

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The hydrothermal-to-surface transfer concept is good and environmentally relevant, but the specific role of rxn_001 in this pathway is chemically underdefined and not clearly connected to the aldehyde/lactaldehyde branch. The surface portion is strong; the hydrothermal contribution appears appended rather than mechanistically integrated.
suggestions: Clarify which hydrothermal products are transferred to the surface branch and how they improve aldehyde availability or concentration
Use formate, acetate, or pyruvate as explicit exported hydrothermal feedstocks instead of bundling keto-acid formation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: As with P6, the environmental handoff is conceptually good, but the hydrothermal precursor chemistry is not well tied to the actual glyceraldehyde/Strecker branch. The glyceraldehyde route itself is plausible but less robust than the lactaldehyde route.
suggestions: Make the hydrothermal input specific and justified
Differentiate between cyanosulfidic glyceraldehyde formation and generic formose-type homologation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pathway is plausible as an analogue to Miller-Urey chemistry because spark discharge can generate HCN, NH3, and aldehydes including threonine precursors. However, the actual reactions encoded are photochemical cyanosulfidic steps rather than spark-discharge steps, so the mapping is indirect rather than explicit.
suggestions: Represent spark-discharge generation as a distinct reaction set rather than relabeling cyanosulfidic photochemistry as an analogue
Note that threonine in discharge chemistry is detected at low abundance in complex mixtures

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This suffers from the same issue as P8: the literature supports spark-discharge threonine production, but the encoded pathway is not truly a discharge mechanism. It remains plausible as a conceptual analogue but should not be presented as a directly validated spark pathway.
suggestions: Separate actual spark-discharge evidence from cyanosulfidic network logic
Add explicit gas-phase precursor generation if retaining the spark-discharge label

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the most problematic pathway. The reverse aldol relationship between glycine, acetaldehyde, and threonine is conceptually known, but the prebiotic forward condensation is specifically identified in the literature as thermodynamically unfavorable and not experimentally demonstrated under purely prebiotic conditions. As encoded, it is too direct and overconfident.
suggestions: Mark this pathway clearly as speculative
Add catalytic assistance, activated glycine equivalents, or concentration mechanisms if retaining it
Avoid treating it as a competitive main route to target accumulation

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a good, well-connected synthesis network with strong literature alignment around the cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde/Strecker chemistry and a useful set of convergent alternatives. Topologically, the network is dense and connected, with clear hubs at HCN, acetaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, lactaldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile. Convergence is good, especially at lactaldehyde and threonine aminonitrile. Environmental interconnection is present, but weaker than claimed because the hydrothermal chemistry is not as rigorously integrated as the surface chemistry. The major strengths are: strong agreement with Ritson-Sutherland/Patel-style chemistry; inclusion of Bucherer-Bergs and HCN polymer hydrolysate alternatives; explicit recognition of threonine fragility; and multiple routes to common intermediates. The principal weaknesses are: rxn_001 is overly compressed and speculative; several pathways terminate at threonine aminonitrile without explicit hydrolysis to threonine; the spark-discharge pathways are analogical rather than mechanistically represented; and the glycine + acetaldehyde aldol route is too speculative to support strongly. Mineral-agent compliance cannot be fully confirmed from the summarized network because specific minerals are not listed for the individual reactions here; this is a notable schema-level concern given the original requirements. Overall, the network is close to approval quality because the core literature-backed pathways are strong, but confidence is reduced by speculative hydrothermal entries and incomplete explicit target-forming steps in some pathways.
